Unlike prior work that treats differential privacy and latency as independent constraints or assumes client homogeneity, this approach integrates device-aware adaptive privacy budget allocation with latency-predictive scheduling, explicitly optimizing the interplay between heterogeneous hardware capabilities, intermittent connectivity, and non-IID data for efficient, private federated aggregation.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory- and communication-efficient personalized federated learning framework that dynamically adapts model compression and aggregation scheduling per heterogeneous edge device to optimize accuracy under strict memory and wireless resource constraints with non-IID data.

BACKGROUND: Current personalized federated learning methods for resource-limited edge devices inadequately address the joint challenges of severe memory constraints and heterogeneous non-IID data distributions, resulting in suboptimal personalization or infeasible memory use. Additionally, existing split federated learning and client scheduling approaches either overlook memory limits or fail to jointly optimize communication overhead and personalization quality under dynamic wireless resource variability.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical adaptive aggregation scheme that integrates lightweight on-device model pruning and quantization with an intelligent aggregation frequency controller driven by real-time device memory and wireless bandwidth monitoring. This controller selectively prioritizes personalized model components for aggregation to balance memory footprint and communication cost dynamically, leveraging a reinforcement learning agent to optimize compression-aggregation trade-offs tailored to each device’s data heterogeneity and resource profile.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on heterogeneous non-IID benchmarks such as CIFAR-10/100 and FEMNIST partitioned by device, simulating constrained edge environments with memory limits (e.g., <100MB RAM) and variable wireless bandwidth. Baselines include state-of-the-art personalized FL aggregation methods, HeteroSFL, and SC client scheduling. Metrics measured are model accuracy, memory usage, communication overhead, and convergence speed.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing work that separately addresses memory efficiency or communication scheduling, our method jointly optimizes personalized aggregation and model compression under both strict memory and wireless resource constraints, dynamically adapting per heterogeneous device and data distribution using a reinforcement learning-driven controller—an approach not explored in prior federated learning frameworks.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a low-latency Bayesian deep emulator for climate model acceleration that integrates adaptive real-time uncertainty calibration through joint optimization of Bayesian inference and model architecture specifically tailored for operational short-term climate projections.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models often fail to simultaneously achieve real-time inference speeds and reliable uncertainty quantification required for operational decision-making, particularly in short-term climate forecasting scenarios. Existing probabilistic models either emphasize uncertainty calibration at the cost of latency or sacrifice calibration for speed, limiting their practical application in accelerated climate simulations and downscaling tasks.

APPROACH: Construct a probabilistic deep neural network architecture combining lightweight convolutional and attention modules optimized for ultra-low latency inference, integrated with a Bayesian inference layer employing amortized variational inference adapted on-the-fly during prediction to maintain calibrated uncertainty under evolving input distributions. The model will be co-trained end-to-end with a latency-aware loss function that penalizes inference time while ensuring uncertainty quality via proper scoring rules. This joint optimization explicitly targets operational short-term climate projections, enabling dynamic real-time uncertainty adjustment.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on high-resolution regional climate datasets derived from downscaled ERA5 reanalysis and CORDEX simulations, benchmarking against state-of-the-art deep climate emulators and Bayesian U-Net models. Metrics include inference latency (milliseconds), uncertainty calibration scores (e.g., Expected Calibration Error, Continuous Ranked Probability Score), and forecast skill scores (e.g., RMSE, correlation) over multiple lead times.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats probabilistic modeling and inference speed as separate optimization tasks, this approach uniquely integrates Bayesian uncertainty quantification with latency-aware architecture and training, delivering real-time calibrated probabilistic predictions specifically tailored for operational short-term climate emulator deployment, thereby bridging the critical gap between speed and uncertainty in practical climate model acceleration.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a memory-optimized sparse neural emulator architecture specifically compressed to operate within sub-50MB memory limits on climate sensor nodes, enabling real-time climate model acceleration with minimal accuracy loss and extended battery life.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning climate emulators focus primarily on minimizing latency or maximizing prediction accuracy, overlooking the critical constraint of memory footprint on edge devices like climate sensor nodes. These nodes require highly memory-efficient models to prolong battery life during long-term field deployments, yet no existing approach explicitly targets sparse model compression optimized for strict memory budgets in this domain.

APPROACH:  
We propose a pipeline combining structured pruning and quantization-aware training to produce sparse deep learning emulators with memory footprints below 50MB, tailored for deployment on climate sensor hardware. The emulator architecture integrates lightweight convolutional blocks with memory-efficient attention mechanisms, calibrated to preserve key climatological feature fidelity. Additionally, a novel memory-aware regularization term is introduced during training to explicitly optimize sparsity patterns that reduce both parameter count and runtime memory usage without degrading emulator accuracy.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on downscaled regional climate model datasets spanning multiple variables (temperature, precipitation) from ensembles of GCM-to-RCM projections, comparing against dense baseline emulators and state-of-the-art sparse compression techniques. Metrics include memory footprint, emulator prediction accuracy (RMSE, pattern correlation), and real-world battery discharge rates measured on deployed sensor node prototypes.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which prioritizes latency or accuracy, this research directly addresses memory as the primary bottleneck by innovating a sparsity-driven compression framework explicitly constrained for sub-50MB deployments on sensor nodes—enabling practical, battery-efficient climate model acceleration in resource-constrained environments for the first time.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-optimized physics-informed neural network (PINN) architecture that enforces local climate physical constraints via lightweight surrogate operators, enabling real-time inference on edge climate sensors with minimal computational overhead.

BACKGROUND: Current PINNs for climate emulation achieve physical fidelity by embedding complex physical constraints directly into the network’s loss function or architecture, resulting in prohibitively high inference latency for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. This latency bottleneck prevents real-time local climate monitoring and rapid decision-making on edge hardware with limited compute capacity, such as low-power microcontrollers or embedded GPUs.

APPROACH: We propose restructuring the enforcement of physical laws by replacing costly differential operators with pre-trained, lightweight surrogate models that approximate physical constraint computations efficiently during inference. The PINN architecture integrates these surrogate operators within a modular framework that dynamically balances physical fidelity and computational cost through latency-aware training objectives. Quantization-aware training and architecture pruning further reduce runtime overhead, enabling deployment on typical edge hardware platforms.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate the model on real-world local climate sensor datasets (temperature, humidity, wind speed) collected from deployed edge devices, benchmarking inference latency, memory footprint, and physical consistency against standard PINNs and black-box deep emulators. Metrics include real-time inference throughput, root-mean-square error, and physics-based residual error on conservation laws.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior PINNs that incur high latency by enforcing physics through costly operators at inference, this approach introduces surrogate physical operators optimized for low-latency edge deployment, enabling a practical tradeoff between physical accuracy and computational efficiency unachieved by existing climate emulators or PINNs.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes deep learning climate emulators for maximal inference throughput and cost-efficiency on cloud batch GPU infrastructure while maintaining acceptable predictive accuracy.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models prioritize accuracy or transferability but overlook system-level deployment constraints such as inference throughput and operational costs in cloud environments. This gap limits the practical scalability of emulators for large-scale, time-sensitive climate projections where cost and speed are critical alongside accuracy.

APPROACH: Formulate NAS as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem targeting inference throughput (samples/sec) and cost-per-sample on cloud GPUs, integrating hardware-aware latency and memory profiling into the search loop. Incorporate accuracy as a soft constraint rather than a primary objective, enabling exploration of architectures that best balance speed, cost, and predictive performance. Use surrogate modeling and early stopping to efficiently evaluate candidate architectures under realistic cloud batch-server conditions.

EXPERIMENT: Benchmark on publicly available regional climate model datasets (e.g., CORDEX) downscaled from GCM outputs, comparing against accuracy-optimized NAS baselines and standard deep learning emulator architectures. Metrics include inference throughput on cloud GPU instances (e.g., AWS/GCP batch servers), cost per forecasted sample, and predictive accuracy (RMSE and spatial correlation).

NOVELTY: Unlike prior approaches focused solely on emulator accuracy or transferability, this work explicitly integrates cloud deployment constraints into NAS, optimizing for throughput and cost-efficiency, thus enabling practical acceleration of climate emulation pipelines at scale without sacrificing moderate accuracy.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a spatio-temporal graph neural network that simultaneously performs probabilistic missing data imputation and multi-horizon global climate state forecasting on irregular, sparse observational graphs, incorporating uncertainty quantification via Bayesian graph layers.

BACKGROUND: Current climate emulators treat missing climate observations and forecasting as separate problems or rely on deterministic interpolation methods that fail to capture data uncertainty and spatiotemporal dependencies in irregularly sampled global climate data. This limits robustness and forecast skill, especially when observational networks are sparse or partially unavailable. There is a critical need for a unified model that integrates imputation and forecasting under uncertainty to accelerate climate model outputs with improved reliability.

APPROACH: We propose a unified spatio-temporal graph neural network architecture that models global climate observations as dynamic, irregular graphs with missing nodes and edges. The model uses Bayesian graph convolutional layers to jointly learn a probabilistic imputation of missing data and multi-step ahead forecasting of climate states, leveraging temporal attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies. The training objective integrates imputation accuracy and forecast skill while propagating uncertainty estimates through Monte Carlo dropout and variational inference techniques.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark datasets including ERA5 reanalysis and CMIP6 simulation outputs with artificially introduced and natural missing data patterns. Baselines include standard interpolation methods (e.g., kriging), state-of-the-art separate imputation and forecasting models, and the winning Elo tournament GNN approach. Metrics comprise imputation RMSE and CRPS (continuous ranked probability score), deterministic and probabilistic forecast skill scores (e.g., RMSE, anomaly correlation coefficient), and calibration metrics for uncertainty quantification.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats imputation and forecasting separately or uses deterministic approaches, this method jointly optimizes both tasks in a single Bayesian spatio-temporal GNN framework on irregular global climate observation graphs, explicitly modeling uncertainty and long-range dependencies to provide more accurate, robust, and uncertainty-aware climate emulators that advance beyond existing deterministic or separate frameworks.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory-optimized generative variational autoencoder with structured latent uncertainty embeddings for edge image classification that enables precise out-of-distribution detection within a strict sub-5MB runtime memory and flash footprint.

BACKGROUND: Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks deployed on edge devices either achieve strong uncertainty estimates at the cost of large memory or sacrifice UQ fidelity to meet resource constraints. Existing generative uncertainty estimators do not explicitly minimize peak RAM and Flash memory usage during inference and training, limiting their applicability for highly resource-constrained edge image recognition tasks requiring reliable OOD detection.

APPROACH: We propose a lightweight conditional variational autoencoder architecture with a hierarchical latent space explicitly partitioned into semantic and uncertainty embeddings, compressed via quantized bottleneck layers and parameterized with low-rank approximations. A memory-aware training regime prunes redundant latent dimensions dynamically and employs mixed-precision training to cap peak memory usage under 5MB. Uncertainty is quantified from the generative posterior variance estimated in the compressed latent space, allowing direct OOD detection by measuring latent reconstruction discrepancies. The model is further optimized for flash constraints via weight sharing and structured sparsity.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on standard OOD benchmarks including CIFAR-10 vs. SVHN, TinyImageNet vs. LSUN, and ImageNet-10 vs. ImageNet-O, profiling peak RAM and Flash usage during training and inference on representative edge hardware. Compare against baseline uncertainty methods including MC Dropout, ensembles, and PSC with metrics of classification accuracy, expected calibration error, AUROC for OOD detection, and memory footprint.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on probabilistic modeling at intermediate layers or active subspace parameter reductions, this approach integrates generative uncertainty estimation directly into a memory- and flash-optimized variational architecture with dynamic latent compression, explicitly balancing OOD detection fidelity against stringent runtime memory constraints for edge deployment.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, hierarchical amortized Bayesian update framework that incrementally refines uncertainty estimates on-device by combining fast local posterior approximations with a learned global uncertainty prior tailored for streaming sensor inputs under strict latency constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current amortized inference methods for on-device Bayesian updates trade off accuracy and speed but do not explicitly leverage hierarchical uncertainty decomposition to enable progressive posterior refinement within strict real-time latency budgets. This limits reliable uncertainty quantification on resource-constrained edge hardware with streaming data, where quick initial uncertainty estimates are needed and can be improved as more data arrives.

APPROACH: Design a two-stage amortized inference network where a shallow, low-compute module produces an immediate, coarse posterior update capturing local uncertainty, followed by a deeper, asynchronous module that refines this update by integrating a learned global uncertainty prior representing long-term sensor behavior. The model dynamically allocates compute based on latency constraints and uncertainty thresholds, enabling progressive posterior updates optimized for small neural architectures on edge devices.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on streaming sensor datasets such as the PhysioNet Sepsis Prediction and Intel Lab Environmental Dataset, comparing against existing amortized inference baselines and Bayesian neural networks using metrics including calibration error, negative log likelihood, latency, and real-time inference accuracy under varying compute budgets.

NOVELTY: Unlike previous amortized inference approaches that produce fixed posterior approximations, this framework uniquely exploits hierarchical uncertainty decomposition and dynamic compute allocation to enable both immediate and refined uncertainty quantification on-device, explicitly balancing latency and uncertainty quality for continual streaming inference on edge hardware.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a Bayesian physics-informed neural network framework embedding explicit PDE constraints from climate physics as probabilistic priors within a hierarchical model to enable calibrated uncertainty quantification under sparse observational data.

BACKGROUND: Existing physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for climate modeling enforce PDE constraints deterministically but lack integrated Bayesian calibration, limiting their ability to produce physically consistent uncertainty estimates when observational data are sparse. This gap restricts reliable uncertainty quantification critical for decision-making in climate predictions under limited data scenarios.

APPROACH: Construct a hierarchical Bayesian model where PDE constraints—expressed as residuals of climate-relevant PDEs—serve as informative probabilistic priors on the neural network outputs and latent states. Use variational inference to jointly estimate the posterior over network weights and PDE residual uncertainty, tightly coupling physical laws with Bayesian uncertainty quantification. Incorporate sparse observational data through likelihood terms and utilize PDE residuals as a soft constraint rather than hard enforcement, enabling principled uncertainty propagation and calibration.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on established climate PDE simulation benchmarks such as the Lorenz 96 system and shallow water equations with sparse and noisy observational data, comparing predictive performance and uncertainty calibration against deterministic PINNs without Bayesian calibration, Bayesian Neural Networks without PDE priors, and classical ensemble methods. Metrics include negative log-likelihood, calibration error, and physical consistency measured by PDE residual statistics.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior PINN UQ models that enforce PDEs deterministically or use heuristic uncertainty measures, this method embeds PDE constraints explicitly as Bayesian priors in a hierarchical model, enabling joint Bayesian calibration of network parameters and physical residuals, thereby producing well-calibrated uncertainties that maintain physical consistency even with sparse data.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a Bayesian Functional Inference framework that decouples and explicitly quantifies epistemic uncertainty from incomplete physical knowledge within deep climate prediction models by integrating partial physical operators as stochastic functionals with uncertainty decomposition layers.

BACKGROUND: Current deep climate models conflate aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, failing to isolate the uncertainty arising specifically from incomplete or approximate physical process representations. This leads to overconfident predictions and poor uncertainty calibration when underlying physics are misspecified or partially known. The open problem is designing a principled uncertainty quantification method that directly attributes epistemic uncertainty to gaps in physical modeling within deep learning climate frameworks.

APPROACH: Introduce a Bayesian functional inference model that embeds partial physical operators as parameterized stochastic functionals within the network architecture, enabling direct inference over functional model discrepancies using variational or MCMC methods. Implement an uncertainty decomposition layer that separates aleatoric noise from epistemic uncertainty induced by physics approximations, leveraging functional Gaussian processes or neural operators to model residual physical uncertainty. Integrate this with a deep climate prediction backbone to enable end-to-end training and uncertainty propagation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark climate simulation datasets with known physics misspecifications (e.g., CMIP6 subsets, simplified Earth system models) and synthetic scenarios with controlled physical process perturbations. Compare against standard Bayesian Neural Networks, Monte Carlo Dropout, and ensemble baselines using uncertainty calibration metrics (e.g., Expected Calibration Error), sharpness, robustness to out-of-distribution perturbations, and predictive accuracy.

NOVELTY: Unlike previous uncertainty quantification methods that treat model uncertainty indirectly in parameter space or latent representations, this approach explicitly models epistemic uncertainty as uncertainty over incomplete physical operators in a functional space, enabling principled decomposition and calibration of uncertainty arising solely from approximate physics in deep climate models.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical latent embedding uncertainty estimation framework that explicitly models and disentangles source-specific distribution shifts via structured latent variables, enabling robust uncertainty quantification and out-of-distribution detection in multi-hospital offline medical imaging.

BACKGROUND: Existing latent embedding uncertainty methods fail to robustly handle distribution shifts caused by heterogeneous data sources—such as multi-hospital medical datasets—leading to miscalibrated uncertainty estimates and poor OOD detection. The open problem is to incorporate explicit modeling of these source-specific shifts into the uncertainty quantification process to achieve reliable performance under distributional variability.

APPROACH: We propose a deep generative model that factorizes the latent embedding space into shared clinical feature embeddings and source-specific shift embeddings, using a hierarchical variational framework that learns structured latent variables representing hospital or source domains. Uncertainty is quantified through Bayesian posterior inference over these disentangled latent factors, with robustness enforced by incorporating adversarial distributional regularization that encourages invariance in shared embeddings while capturing source-specific variations separately. This allows calibrated uncertainty estimates and effective OOD detection by contrasting learned source embeddings.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on publicly available multi-center medical imaging datasets (e.g., MIMIC-CXR, ISIC skin lesion data from multiple clinics) comparing against state-of-the-art latent embedding uncertainty methods, PSC, and active subspace methods. Metrics include uncertainty calibration error, out-of-distribution detection AUROC, and robustness under synthetic and real distribution shifts across hospitals.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior latent-space uncertainty techniques that treat embeddings monolithically or ignore source heterogeneity, this method explicitly disentangles distribution shifts into structured latent variables with adversarial regularization, enabling robust, calibrated uncertainty quantification that generalizes to unseen hospital domains in offline medical imaging contexts.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a domain-adaptive, sparse transformer-based foundation model that jointly embeds multimodal single-cell and population-scale genomics data with explicit domain adversarial training to achieve robust cross-protocol and cross-population generalization under severe domain shifts.

BACKGROUND: Current foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology struggle to generalize across heterogeneous sequencing protocols and diverse populations due to domain shifts, limiting their utility for inclusive biomedical discovery. Existing approaches lack scalable architectures that unify multimodal data embedding while actively adapting to domain discrepancies, resulting in poor robustness on held-out populations and protocol variations.

APPROACH: We propose a scalable sparse transformer architecture pretrained on millions of single-cell transcriptomes, epigenomes, and population-scale genotypes jointly, incorporating domain adversarial neural networks to minimize domain-specific features and produce domain-invariant embeddings. The model integrates cross-modal attention mechanisms that learn shared biological representations across data types while explicitly adapting to protocol and population-specific shifts through adversarial loss optimization, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen sequencing platforms and populations.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate embedding quality and cross-domain robustness on large-scale consortium datasets including multi-ethnic single-cell atlases (e.g., Human Cell Atlas, 10x Multiome), population genomics cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, gnomAD), and held-out minority populations and novel sequencing protocols. Metrics include clustering purity, classification accuracy on cell type and phenotype prediction tasks, domain generalization scores, and embedding stability across protocols compared to state-of-the-art baseline models without domain adaptation.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and cross-modal genomics embeddings, our method explicitly integrates domain adversarial training with sparse transformer scalability to robustly handle extreme domain shifts across both populations and sequencing protocols, enabling comprehensive, unified foundation models that generalize broadly to diverse real-world biomedical data.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a causal representation learning framework that extracts robust, interpretable disease phenotypes from severely limited and confounded single-cell patient data by integrating weak supervision, domain priors, and counterfactual inference within a foundation model architecture.

BACKGROUND: Single-cell clinical datasets for disease phenotyping are often plagued by extreme data scarcity, strong biological noise, and confounding factors such as batch effects and patient heterogeneity, limiting the ability of existing methods to learn stable and clinically meaningful representations. Current single-cell foundation models and representation learning approaches typically require large cohorts and lack systematic mechanisms to disentangle confounders from causal disease signals, which undermines phenotype reproducibility and downstream clinical utility.

APPROACH: We propose a foundation model-based architecture that incorporates (1) weak supervision from limited clinical annotations and domain knowledge encoded as structured priors, (2) disentangled representation learning to separate confounding variation from disease-related signals using adversarial and invariant risk minimization objectives, and (3) counterfactual data augmentation via generative modeling to simulate intervention on confounders for causal effect estimation. The model jointly learns cell-level embeddings that are explicitly regularized to capture causally relevant phenotypes, enabling robust cross-patient generalization despite data scarcity.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multiple publicly available and newly curated clinical single-cell datasets from diverse disease contexts with severe confounding (e.g., lupus nephritis, cancer immunotherapy cohorts) using phenotype reproducibility metrics, clinical outcome prediction accuracy, and alignment with expert annotations; compare against state-of-the-art single-cell foundation models, unsupervised embedding methods, and confounder-robust representation learning baselines.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell foundation models that focus on scale or multimodality, this work explicitly targets causal disentanglement under extreme scarcity and confounding by integrating weak supervision and counterfactual reasoning within a unified framework, enabling robust disease phenotype extraction and clinical interpretability unattainable by current methods.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a Causal Multimodal Graph Neural Network that jointly models experimental perturbations, multi-omic single-cell profiles, and latent confounders to robustly infer context-specific gene regulatory networks with causal directionality.

BACKGROUND: Current causal inference methods for gene regulatory network (GRN) discovery from single-cell perturbation data fail to integrate multi-omic modalities (e.g., chromatin accessibility, protein levels) and often do not explicitly model latent confounders and experimental interventions within a unified causal framework. This limits accurate disentanglement of direct causal effects from indirect or confounded associations, especially in complex high-dimensional perturbation screens with unknown confounders.

APPROACH: We propose a Causal Multimodal Graph Neural Network (CM-GNN) that encodes single-cell multi-omic states and perturbation conditions as nodes and edges in a causal graph, explicitly modeling intervention targets and propagating causal effects through learned latent representations. The model incorporates variational inference to capture unknown confounders and disentangles direct causal regulatory edges from spurious correlations by leveraging multi-omic data integration and intervention-aware message passing. The architecture jointly optimizes causal discovery and multi-modal representation learning to recover directed GRNs specific to cellular contexts.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate CM-GNN on publicly available large-scale single-cell perturbation datasets with matched multi-omic profiles (e.g., Perturb-seq combined with ATAC-seq and CITE-seq), comparing against leading causal and non-causal GRN inference baselines using metrics including precision-recall of known regulatory edges, causal direction accuracy, and robustness to simulated confounders.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior causal graph neural networks that consider only gene expression and fail to model latent confounders explicitly, CM-GNN integrates multi-omic single-cell data and perturbation-aware causal discovery within a single foundation architecture, enabling robust and interpretable inference of directed gene regulatory networks under complex confounding and diverse cellular states.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable distributed training framework that integrates advanced memory compression techniques with model and data parallelism to efficiently train foundation models on multimodal population-scale genomics and single-cell datasets exceeding millions of samples.

BACKGROUND: Current foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology face critical scalability bottlenecks when applied to population-scale heterogeneous data comprising millions of samples due to prohibitive memory requirements and inefficient compute distribution. Existing frameworks either optimize for large-scale model parallelism or memory efficiency independently but fail to jointly address both challenges, limiting the ability to train high-capacity models on the full scope of available genomics and single-cell data.

APPROACH: Implement a unified distributed training pipeline that combines model parallelism (e.g., tensor and pipeline parallelism) with state-of-the-art memory-efficient data representations such as quantized embeddings and sparse input encodings, dynamically compressed during training to reduce per-node memory footprint. Incorporate asynchronous data loading and hybrid precision training to further optimize throughput. The system will support multimodal inputs (e.g., genomic sequences, single-cell transcriptomes, epigenetic profiles) and heterogeneous batch scheduling to maintain high GPU utilization across compute clusters.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on large-scale public population genomics datasets (e.g., UK Biobank whole-genome sequences) combined with large single-cell atlases (e.g., Human Cell Atlas), measuring training throughput, GPU memory consumption, and predictive performance on downstream tasks such as genotype-phenotype association and cell-type classification. Baselines include existing distributed training frameworks without joint memory optimizations and single-node training with memory compression alone.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which treats scalability or memory efficiency in isolation, this approach uniquely integrates distributed training strategies with dynamic memory compression specifically tailored for multimodal population-scale genomics and single-cell data, enabling training of foundation models beyond current size and heterogeneity limits while preserving predictive fidelity.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a neuro-symbolic foundation model architecture that integrates inherently interpretable symbolic reasoning modules with pretrained single-cell embeddings to produce clinically actionable, explanation-first diagnostic predictions from patient single-cell data.

BACKGROUND: Current single-cell foundation models prioritize predictive accuracy but rely on black-box neural representations whose reasoning processes are opaque, hindering clinical adoption under regulatory requirements for transparent decision-making. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches apply explainability post hoc rather than embedding it as a core design principle, limiting interpretability and trustworthiness in clinical single-cell diagnostics.

APPROACH: We propose a modular neuro-symbolic model where pretrained single-cell foundation embeddings feed into explicitly structured symbolic reasoning chains expressed as differentiable logic programs constrained to use clinically meaningful features and cell-type markers. The model enforces interpretable intermediate representations via neuro-symbolic bottlenecks and integrates a learnable neuro-symbolic interface layer to translate single-cell latent features into symbolic predicates. Training jointly optimizes diagnostic accuracy and quantitative explainability objectives, utilizing constrained explanation budgets to ensure sparse, human-comprehensible reasoning paths.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multi-cohort single-cell clinical datasets from cancer and autoimmune disease studies with curated diagnostic labels; compare against black-box single-cell FMs and post-hoc explainability baselines. Metrics include diagnostic accuracy, fidelity of explanations to decision logic, sparsity and complexity of symbolic chains, and clinician-rated interpretability scores.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and neuro-symbolic attempts, this work enforces explainability as a primary architectural constraint via neuro-symbolic bottlenecks and integrated symbolic predicates aligned to clinical semantics, thereby ensuring explanations are generated intrinsically during prediction rather than extracted post hoc, directly addressing clinical regulatory demands for interpretable single-cell diagnostics.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a decentralized, self-adaptive emergent communication protocol that maintains coordination and message integrity in multi-agent disaster response networks experiencing highly dynamic topology changes and extreme connectivity intermittency.

BACKGROUND: Existing emergent communication protocols for multi-agent systems typically assume relatively stable or slowly changing network topologies, limiting their applicability in disaster response scenarios where network nodes frequently disconnect and reconnect unpredictably. Current methods lack mechanisms for continuous adaptation under severe bandwidth constraints and absence of centralized control, resulting in degraded coordination and communication robustness in realistic disaster environments.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-layer protocol combining (1) a biologically inspired dynamic vocabulary evolution mechanism that allows agents to locally create, prune, and re-map communication symbols in response to link changes, (2) an intermittent connectivity-aware message encoding scheme that leverages coded redundancy and partial message reconstruction to mitigate packet loss, and (3) decentralized consensus algorithms for real-time alignment of emergent language semantics without global synchronization. Agents learn via reinforcement over simulated disaster scenarios to optimize communication resilience and task performance under bandwidth and topology constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate using high-fidelity network simulators modeling urban disaster scenarios with mobile agents and random link failures; benchmark against recent emergent communication baselines optimized for static networks using metrics including message delivery success rate, language stability, coordination task success, and bandwidth efficiency.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior emergent communication work that assumes static or stable connectivity or relies on centralized training, this method explicitly integrates topology dynamics and intermittent links into the language evolution process with decentralized consensus, yielding robust, continuously adaptive communication protocols tuned for disaster response network realities.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a fault-resilient graph neural network communication framework integrating real-time fault detection and adaptive message correction to maintain vehicle platoon coherence under dynamic topology changes and severe communication faults.

BACKGROUND: Existing graph neural network (GNN) approaches for vehicle platooning assume reliable communication and complete vehicle state data, failing to handle noisy, missing, or corrupted messages that frequently occur in realistic vehicular networks. This results in degraded platoon stability, increased collision risk, and slow recovery after communication faults, which remain unsolved in highly dynamic, decentralized multi-agent systems.

APPROACH: We propose a GNN-based communication architecture that incorporates an embedded fault detection module using uncertainty estimation and anomaly scoring on incoming messages, combined with a fault-tolerant aggregation mechanism that selectively weights or reconstructs messages based on detected faults. Additionally, adaptive message correction layers leverage spatiotemporal redundancy in vehicle state data to impute missing or noisy inputs before aggregation, enabling the platoon GNN to maintain coherent, robust embeddings for control decisions despite intermittent communication failures.

EXPERIMENT: Simulate vehicle platoons in high-fidelity traffic simulators augmented with controlled packet loss, message corruption, and adversarial communication faults; compare the proposed method against state-of-the-art GNN communication baselines using metrics including platoon cohesion (inter-vehicle distance variance), collision rate, and recovery time after faults.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior GNN methods that treat communication channels as fault-free, this framework explicitly integrates fault detection and adaptive correction within the message aggregation pipeline, enabling robust emergent communication under realistic network degradation — a capability absent from existing multi-agent emergent communication and vehicle platooning research.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a unified neuro-symbolic emergent communication framework that dynamically adapts protocol complexity and enforces formal safety constraints in decentralized multi-agent autonomous fleets operating under stringent real-time latency and bandwidth limitations.

BACKGROUND: Existing neuro-symbolic protocols for multi-agent coordination either statically integrate symbolic rule enforcement with neural adaptation or focus separately on latency or symbolic reasoning, limiting their applicability in complex, dynamic environments with varying communication constraints. The open problem is enabling autonomous vehicle fleets to dynamically tune their emergent communication protocols to maintain safety and coordination effectiveness while strictly adhering to latency and bandwidth constraints in real time.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical neuro-symbolic communication architecture where agents learn to modulate the granularity of symbolic messages combined with adaptive neural embeddings conditioned on current network latency and environmental complexity. A formal safety verifier module is embedded within the communication loop to enforce rule compliance at runtime, pruning unsafe message constructs before transmission. This adaptive protocol dynamically balances communication overhead and interpretability, enabling the fleet to maintain coordination performance and safety under fluctuating latency and bandwidth conditions.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate the framework using multi-vehicle simulation platforms modeling urban traffic scenarios with variable network conditions and dynamic obstacles. Metrics include communication latency, bandwidth usage, safety violation rates, coordination success rates, and protocol adaptability compared against fixed neuro-symbolic baselines and pure neural or symbolic communication methods.

NOVELTY: This work goes beyond prior static neuro-symbolic communication by introducing a dynamic protocol complexity modulation mechanism integrated with online formal safety verification under real-time latency and bandwidth constraints, addressing a critical gap in emergent communication for safety-critical autonomous fleet coordination.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a robust, adaptive error-correcting temporal coding framework leveraging biologically inspired spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) for emergent communication in adversarial multi-agent systems operating in dynamic, noisy environments.

BACKGROUND: Current emergent communication methods in multi-agent systems lack resilience against adversarial temporal noise and spoofing common in hostile or competitive environments, such as high-frequency trading or swarm robotics under interference. While prior work introduces error-correcting temporal codes for trading agents, these codes rely on static schemes that fail to adapt to evolving adversarial strategies and environmental dynamics, limiting communication fidelity and coordination robustness.

APPROACH: We propose integrating an online adaptive error-correction mechanism based on spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) into temporal coding protocols, enabling agents to dynamically recalibrate code parameters to counteract observed adversarial noise patterns. The method combines (1) temporal code generation optimized for minimal redundancy and maximal temporal distinctiveness, (2) STDP-driven adaptive synaptic weights to detect and correct corrupted spike sequences in real-time, and (3) a feedback loop where agents exchange meta-signals indicating confidence and error estimates to collaboratively maintain communication integrity under adversarial conditions.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on synthetic adversarial multi-agent simulations modeled on high-frequency trading and swarm navigation tasks with injected temporal noise and spoofing attacks. Compare communication fidelity, error rates, and task performance against fixed error-correcting temporal codes and non-adaptive baselines using metrics such as bit error rate, task success rate, and communication overhead.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing approaches that apply static error-correcting temporal codes or optimize emergent communication without adversarial adaptation, this work introduces biologically plausible STDP-based adaptation directly into temporal coding, enabling agents to learn and counter evolving adversarial patterns in real-time, thereby significantly improving robustness and task performance in hostile multi-agent environments.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-aware, dynamic graph attention network with multi-modal encoding and adaptive symbol compression to enable robust emergent communication protocols that scale efficiently under highly volatile vehicle-to-vehicle network topologies.

BACKGROUND: Existing V2V communication methods assume relatively stable network graphs or rely on static attention mechanisms that fail to adapt quickly to rapid topology changes in autonomous vehicle platoons, leading to increased latency and degraded situational awareness. The open problem is how to design communication protocols that dynamically prioritize critical environmental information and adjust encoding schemes in real-time to maintain low-latency symbol transmission despite frequent connectivity disruptions.

APPROACH: We propose a Multi-Modal Dynamic Graph Attention Network (MD-GAT) that integrates heterogeneous sensory inputs (e.g., LiDAR, radar, camera) into a unified latent space while dynamically updating attention weights based on instantaneous network connectivity graphs. This network incorporates a latency-constrained symbol compression module that adapts encoding granularity per message priority and current channel conditions. The communication protocol leverages reinforcement learning with an explicit latency penalty to prioritize timely transmission of critical messages and prune redundant updates in evolving topologies.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate MD-GAT on simulated autonomous vehicle platoon datasets with diverse, rapidly changing network topologies under varying traffic densities and environmental conditions. Baselines include static graph attention models, standard multi-agent communication protocols without adaptive compression, and prior latency-agnostic V2V communication algorithms. Metrics comprise end-to-end communication delay, task success rate in cooperative navigation and obstacle avoidance, and bandwidth utilization.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior emergent communication works that assume static or slowly changing network graphs or use fixed encoding schemes, our method explicitly models dynamic topologies with multi-modal attention and integrates latency constraints directly into the communication and encoding processes, enabling real-time prioritization and compression that substantially improve robustness and efficiency in volatile V2V environments.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a layer-wise, dynamics-informed method to identify and track evolving semantic energy basins in transformer residual stream trajectories, linking attractor states not only to static semantic clusters but also to their formation and transformation across model depth.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing work maps semantic energy basins in static transformer embeddings aggregated over large corpora but does not capture how these basins form, evolve, merge, or split across layers in the residual stream dynamics. Understanding the mechanistic progression of semantic attractors through the network is an open problem critical to linking model internals with emergent linguistic abstractions and compositionality.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework combining energy landscape analysis with dynamical systems tools to analyze transformer residual streams layer-by-layer. For each layer, we construct energy functions over token embeddings and identify semantic basins via basin-hopping and local minima finding algorithms. We then develop a basin-tracking algorithm that aligns and maps these basins across consecutive layers, revealing attractor evolution. We formalize semantic basin transitions as state changes in a Markov graph and interpret these transitions in terms of linguistic phenomena such as disambiguation or phrase composition.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on large-scale static text corpora processed by pretrained transformers (e.g., GPT-2, RoBERTa) using human-annotated semantic categories (WordNet synsets, topic labels) as ground truth. Measure basin clustering purity, stability, and layer-wise transition consistency. Baselines include static basin identification without dynamics and clustering on raw embeddings. Additional evaluation on syntactic/semantic probing tasks validates linguistic interpretability.

NOVELTY:  
This work meaningfully extends prior static semantic basin identification by integrating mechanistic interpretability with the temporal dimension of layerwise transformer dynamics. Unlike prior analyses focusing on singular vectors or residual unit dynamics in isolation, this method explicitly models how semantic attractors emerge and transform across layers, providing a mechanistic bridge between static semantic clustering and the dynamical system perspective of transformer processing.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Extract robust, generalizable symbolic programs from sparse and noisy transformer activations by integrating noise-aware abstraction layers and minimal supervision, specifically designed for low-resource language and domain settings.

BACKGROUND: Existing mechanistic interpretability methods struggle to reliably extract symbolic, human-understandable programs from transformer model internal states when data is sparse or activations are noisy, especially in low-resource language or domain contexts. Current approaches typically require abundant data or clean activations, limiting applicability to real-world, low-data scenarios where interpretability is most critical.

APPROACH: We propose a novel framework that combines noise-robust abstraction modules with a minimal supervision paradigm tailored for sparse, noisy activations. The method leverages a singular vector decomposition-inspired denoising layer to isolate stable activation subspaces, followed by a hybrid symbolic extraction pipeline that incrementally builds generalized program representations using weak supervision signals. This approach explicitly models and mitigates activation noise and scarcity by learning abstractions that are invariant across activation perturbations and training set variations.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on multiple low-resource benchmarks including underrepresented languages (e.g., Guarani, Yoruba) and domain-specific corpora with limited labeled data; compare to state-of-the-art symbolic extraction baselines on held-out task generalization accuracy, robustness to synthetic noise injection in activations, and interpretability quantified by program complexity and fidelity metrics.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that relies on dense supervision or assumes clean activations, our method explicitly incorporates noise-robust abstraction and minimal supervision mechanisms that enable reliable symbolic program extraction in severely data- and signal-constrained settings, advancing mechanistic interpretability into realistic low-resource applications.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical density-based clustering framework to identify multi-scale mechanistic motifs in transformer activation trajectories, enabling detection of nested and temporally overlapping mechanistic phases missed by existing flat clustering methods.

BACKGROUND: Existing mechanistic interpretability approaches that use density-based clustering for offline model debugging detect subtle mechanistic phases by identifying complex-shaped or rare patterns in transformer activations. However, these methods operate on a single clustering scale, limiting their ability to capture hierarchical or nested phases and temporal overlaps in transformer computations, which are common in deep, multi-layer models. This leaves multi-scale mechanistic structures and their interactions unexplored.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical density-based clustering algorithm that recursively segments full offline activation histories of transformers into nested clusters representing mechanistic motifs at multiple temporal and representational scales. This method combines HDBSCAN with temporal continuity constraints and introduces a novel temporal smoothing regularizer that respects layer-wise and residual stream dynamics. The resulting multi-level clustering uncovers both broad phases and fine-grained sub-phases, capturing overlapping or embedded mechanistic patterns. We integrate singular vector decomposition within each cluster to disentangle superposed computations and correlate motifs with downstream error cases.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on transformer models trained for language modeling (e.g., GPT-2 medium) and time series classification (per Mechanistic Interpretability for Transformer-based Time Series Classification). Metrics include clustering stability, silhouette scores, and mutual information with known error occurrences. Baselines include flat HDBSCAN as in previous work and centroid-based algorithms. Further qualitative mechanistic validation involves visualizing phase motifs across layers and relating them to model behavior on benchmark datasets like WikiText-103 and UCR time series.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that applies flat density-based clustering to identify mechanistic phases, this method explicitly models hierarchical and overlapping phase structures, revealing multi-scale dynamics within transformer activations. It also uniquely integrates temporal regularization and singular vector analysis within clusters, enabling dissection of nested and superposed computations—advancing mechanistic interpretability beyond current single-scale or component-centric perspectives.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-constrained incremental causal intervention framework that probes transformer activations in real-time on edge devices by adaptively selecting minimal intervention subsets to maximize mechanistic insight without violating strict inference latency budgets.

BACKGROUND: Existing mechanistic interpretability methods for transformers typically perform offline, bulk analyses of model activations, ignoring the strict latency and resource constraints of edge hardware where real-time inference is critical. There is no existing method that systematically balances causal intervention granularity with runtime latency to enable live, incremental interpretability probing on edge platforms.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive incremental causal intervention method that dynamically selects small subsets of transformer activations—attention heads, MLP units, or residual stream components—for targeted interventions during live inference. The method incorporates a latency-aware scheduler that constrains intervention complexity to meet predefined real-time budgets by dynamically adjusting intervention granularity and frequency. Interventions are designed as lightweight causal perturbations (e.g., minimal activation ablations or controlled input substitutions) that probe mechanistic effects incrementally, enabling stepwise interpretability refinement without bulk offline computation.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate the framework on representative edge hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, ARM-based SoCs) using transformer models fine-tuned for natural language tasks (e.g., SST-2 sentiment classification) and time series classification benchmarks. Metrics include causal interpretability gain (measured by downstream attribution fidelity and explanation consistency), measured latency overhead during live inference, and real-time throughput. Baselines include offline causal intervention methods and naive bulk interventions adapted to edge hardware without latency constraints.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that focus on offline or high-latency mechanistic analyses, our method explicitly integrates latency constraints into the causal intervention process, enabling mechanistic interpretability probes during live inference on edge devices. This real-time, incremental approach to causal intervention addresses a critical deployment gap and extends interpretability methodologies beyond static, resource-unaware settings.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory-constrained causal mediation framework that compresses causal representations and applies approximate inference techniques to enable mechanistic interpretability of transformer models within a strict 50 MB RAM runtime limit on edge devices.

BACKGROUND: Existing mechanistic interpretability methods for transformers rely on large memory footprints which prohibit their deployment and causal analysis during inference on resource-limited edge devices. There is currently no framework that efficiently performs causal mediation analysis of transformer internals under tight memory constraints while maintaining interpretability and inference accuracy.

APPROACH: We design a lightweight causal mediation pipeline that compresses intermediate causal representations using sparse low-rank factorization and quantization to minimize memory usage. Approximate inference algorithms based on variational message passing are employed to estimate mediation effects without storing full activations. This framework is integrated into transformer inference to simultaneously produce interpretable causal insights with less than 50 MB peak RAM usage.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on standard edge transformer benchmarks for NLP and time-series tasks (e.g., EdgeGLUE, UCI HAR) measuring peak memory consumption, fidelity of causal mediation against full-memory baselines, and impact on downstream inference accuracy.

NOVELTY: This is the first mechanistic interpretability framework to explicitly optimize and validate causal mediation under stringent memory constraints at runtime, substantially surpassing prior work which assumes abundant memory and offline analysis, thus enabling real-time edge interpretability.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Meta-learn a dual-objective adaptation prior that simultaneously minimizes policy initialization latency and maximizes intrinsic task inference efficiency by integrating a learned rapid context encoder with a policy parameter generator, enabling truly instantaneous zero-shot generalization on novel tasks without any environment interaction.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing meta-learned adaptation priors optimize for rapid policy initialization but neglect the critical bottleneck of inferring latent task context from limited or no observations, which impedes truly zero-shot generalization. Current methods either assume perfect or slow context inference, leading to suboptimal deployment speed when encountering out-of-distribution tasks. The open problem is achieving both near-instantaneous policy instantiation and immediate, accurate task identification under zero environment interaction.

APPROACH:  
We propose to jointly meta-learn (1) a lightweight, amortized context encoder trained to extract task representations directly from minimal offline task specification or prior demonstrations, and (2) a conditional policy parameter generator that produces near-optimal policies conditioned on encoded task embeddings with negligible initialization latency. Training uses a bi-level meta-objective that explicitly penalizes both policy initialization delay and task inference uncertainty, enforced via a differentiable task similarity loss and a latency regularizer. The integration enables agents to deploy effective policies instantly upon task receipt, without trial-and-error adaptation.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on out-of-distribution continuous control benchmarks (e.g., variants of Meta-World and DM Control with unseen dynamics and goals), measuring zero-shot episodic return and total policy deployment latency (including context inference time). Baselines include the winning Meta-Learned Adaptation Priors method, context-aware adaptation methods, and offline-only zero-shot RL approaches. Metrics focus on cumulative reward without further training and end-to-end deployment speed.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that optimizes only policy initialization speed or assumes static context representations, we co-optimize rapid, accurate task inference with minimal policy initialization latency, closing the gap between context understanding and policy deployment to achieve genuine zero-shot generalization in RL. This explicit dual-objective meta-learning of both task inference and policy generation is unprecedented in zero-shot adaptation literature.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Integrate energy-aware meta-learning with a dynamic neural architecture search to enable zero-shot reinforcement learning adaptation of personalized edge assistants under stringent energy constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current zero-shot RL methods for edge personal assistants fail to jointly optimize energy consumption and user-specific adaptation latency, relying on static or compute-constrained architectures that limit flexibility and efficiency. The open problem is how to design a framework that dynamically reconfigures model architectures during meta-learned adaptation to rapidly and efficiently personalize assistants in unseen user contexts while explicitly minimizing energy use.

APPROACH: We propose an energy-efficient meta-learning framework that incorporates a differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) module directly into the meta-adaptation process, enabling dynamic adjustment of model complexity and topology conditioned on user interaction context. The NAS is guided by a composite loss that balances recommendation accuracy, adaptation latency, and real-time energy consumption measured in joules, allowing the edge assistant to automatically trade off performance and energy budget per user. This joint optimization is performed end-to-end during meta-training, producing architectures that generalize zero-shot to novel users by quickly converging to personalized, energy-optimal configurations without additional fine-tuning.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on realistic user simulation datasets reflecting diverse preferences and interaction patterns, comparing against static-architecture meta-learners and compute-constrained baselines. Metrics include zero-shot adaptation latency (seconds), cumulative energy consumption (joules) on representative edge hardware, and recommendation accuracy (e.g., top-K hit rate), under varying energy budgets.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that constrains compute or learns static architectures, this method uniquely integrates energy consumption as a first-class optimization objective into the meta-learning and architecture search loop, enabling dynamic architecture reconfiguration for zero-shot RL personalized assistants. This approach meaningfully extends state-of-the-art by jointly optimizing energy efficiency and rapid adaptation in a zero-shot setting, a capability absent in existing meta-RL and zero-shot generalization frameworks.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical compositional symbolic framework that explicitly learns disentangled dialogue action and user intent primitives, enabling zero-shot generalization by dynamically recomposing these primitives into novel dialogue plans under uncertain, unseen conversational goals.

BACKGROUND: Existing approaches to zero-shot generalization in open-ended dialogue planning struggle to robustly handle unseen dialogue types and ambiguous user intents because they either rely on end-to-end learned policies or monolithic symbolic models that lack fine-grained compositional structure. The specific open problem is how to systematically represent and reuse elemental dialogue actions and latent user intents as modular, interpretable primitives that can be flexibly recombined to generalize zero-shot to novel dialogue scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical symbolic representation that disentangles dialogue management into two core primitive sets: (1) dialogue action primitives encoding atomic communicative acts abstracted from natural language, and (2) latent user intent primitives learned via unsupervised clustering of dialogue context embeddings. A compositional symbolic planner dynamically assembles these primitives by matching inferred user intent primitives to suitable dialogue action sequences without any fine-tuning. The system incorporates a probabilistic symbolic reasoning module to manage uncertainty in user intent and guide planning decisions, facilitating zero-shot transfer across diverse, unseen dialogue tasks.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on established dialogue simulators such as MultiWOZ and DialCrowd extended with novel, unseen domain scenarios and user intents. Baselines include the state-of-the-art compositional symbolic model (the tournament winner) and recent zero-shot RL dialogue policies. Metrics are task completion rate, user satisfaction proxy scores, and adaptability measured by success on held-out dialogue types without policy retraining.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats symbolic models as monolithic or end-to-end learned policies as opaque, our method explicitly disentangles and learns modular user intent and dialogue action primitives, enabling principled symbolic recomposition. This hierarchical disentanglement combined with probabilistic reasoning over latent intent primitives meaningfully advances zero-shot generalization capabilities beyond current compositional symbolic or reinforcement learning approaches.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop an online incremental causal discovery framework that dynamically updates compact causal models on edge robotics platforms to enable zero-shot manipulation of novel objects under real-time compute and memory constraints.

BACKGROUND: Existing zero-shot manipulation methods rely heavily on offline batch causal discovery, which cannot adapt to new environmental changes or unseen objects encountered by resource-limited edge robots. This limits zero-shot generalization in real-world scenarios where continuous updates to causal models are required under strict runtime constraints.

APPROACH: We propose an online incremental causal discovery algorithm that continuously refines a sparse causal graph by integrating streaming sensory and interaction data with a lightweight Bayesian update scheme optimized for edge devices. This causal graph conditions a policy network trained with zero-shot generalization objectives to perform novel object manipulations without retraining. Resource-aware pruning and quantization techniques are incorporated to maintain model efficiency.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on a suite of simulated and physical edge robot platforms manipulating diverse unseen object categories with varying dynamics. Metrics include zero-shot task success rate, runtime memory footprint, compute latency, and model update frequency, compared against offline causal discovery baselines and state-of-the-art zero-shot RL policies.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior offline or context-inferring zero-shot RL methods, this approach enables continuous real-time causal model updates directly on edge robots, tightly integrating incremental causal discovery with zero-shot policy execution under strict resource constraints, thereby advancing practical zero-shot manipulation in dynamic real-world settings.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Develop a resource-aware incremental world model adaptation framework that enables fully unsupervised zero-shot policy generalization on edge devices by dynamically balancing model complexity and adaptation fidelity under strict computational and memory constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current zero-shot generalization methods rely on static or cloud-updated world models that fail to adapt to evolving dynamics or sensor drift on edge hardware, leading to degraded performance over long-term deployments. The open problem is enabling continual, unsupervised adaptation of compact world models directly on resource-constrained embedded devices to sustain zero-shot control without retraining or cloud dependency.

APPROACH: We propose an incremental world model adaptation algorithm that ingests streaming sensor data on edge devices, selectively updates latent dynamics representations using lightweight variational inference, and incorporates a novel resource-aware controller that dynamically trades off model complexity and prediction accuracy given real-time compute and memory budgets. The framework integrates an adaptive uncertainty quantification module to trigger incremental policy adjustments zero-shot, maintaining stable control under nonstationary conditions without explicit supervision or cloud access.

EXPERIMENT: Validate on long-term RL control tasks in embedded robotics and IoT simulation testbeds with diverse environmental shifts, comparing zero-shot performance, adaptation speed, energy consumption, and model footprint against static world models and cloud-dependent adaptation baselines using cumulative reward, model likelihood, and resource utilization metrics.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on offline or cloud-updated world models and policy context conditioning, this approach directly addresses unsupervised world model adaptation on edge hardware with principled resource trade-offs, enabling sustained zero-shot policy generalization under real-world embedded constraints not considered by existing methods.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive streaming persistent homology algorithm with provable, dynamically adjustable latency bounds that optimize topological anomaly detection on edge sensor data under varying noise and resource constraints.

BACKGROUND: Existing streaming persistent homology algorithms provide fixed latency guarantees that do not adapt to changes in data noise levels or sensor resource availability, limiting their effectiveness for timely anomaly detection in real-world edge sensor deployments. The open problem is designing an approach that maintains strict, provable latency bounds while dynamically adjusting computational effort to optimize accuracy and responsiveness in non-stationary streaming environments.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical sketching framework that incrementally computes persistence diagrams using multi-resolution topological summaries, combined with an online noise-adaptive filtering module that estimates data uncertainty to modulate the resolution and update frequency. This enables provable upper bounds on end-to-end latency that tighten or relax dynamically based on runtime noise and computational load, ensuring near real-time updates with controlled topological approximation error. The method is implemented with lightweight data structures suitable for deployment on edge sensor hardware.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate using real-world and synthetic multi-sensor streaming datasets exhibiting variable noise and anomaly rates, comparing latency, topological accuracy (bottleneck/Wasserstein distances to offline baselines), and anomaly detection F1 scores against fixed-latency streaming persistent homology and baseline incremental methods.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that enforces static latency guarantees, our method introduces a provably adaptive latency mechanism driven by online noise estimation, enabling more efficient and accurate streaming topological summaries tailored to dynamic edge sensing conditions.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop a streaming algorithm for approximate persistent homology that dynamically compresses intermediate simplicial complexes using adaptive simplification and sketching techniques to minimize memory usage on edge devices while providing explicit tunable bounds on topological error.

BACKGROUND: Existing persistent homology algorithms require large memory and offline access to entire datasets, making them infeasible for edge devices processing continuous noisy sensor streams with stringent memory constraints. Prior approximate methods either assume ample memory or focus on static data, failing to address the challenge of maintaining topological summaries under streaming, noisy, and dynamic environmental conditions with explicit accuracy-memory trade-offs.

APPROACH: We propose a memory-adaptive streaming persistent homology algorithm that incrementally updates topological features by maintaining compressed representations of evolving simplicial complexes via adaptive edge collapse and randomized sketching of boundary matrices. The method incorporates a tunable error parameter controlling the granularity of simplification and the rank of sketches, enabling explicit trade-offs between memory footprint and approximation accuracy. Noise robustness is ensured through online denoising filters integrated with the topological updates.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark sensor stream datasets including wearable motion sensors and environmental IoT data, comparing memory use, approximation error of persistence diagrams, and robustness of detected features under varying noise levels against state-of-the-art offline and streaming persistent homology methods.

NOVELTY: This method explicitly integrates adaptive compression and sketching into streaming persistent homology computation, providing the first framework with provable tunable memory-accuracy trade-offs tailored for memory-limited edge devices processing noisy, real-time sensor streams, surpassing prior approximate offline or high-memory streaming approaches.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop a streaming, memory-constrained Mapper algorithm that incrementally updates topological summaries of high-dimensional sensory data on autonomous vehicle edge devices by combining adaptive micro-clustering with reservoir sampling and hierarchical cover construction within a fixed 1GB memory footprint.

BACKGROUND: Real-time topological data analysis (TDA) on autonomous vehicles requires Mapper implementations that operate under strict memory constraints while processing continuous, high-dimensional sensor streams. Existing memory-constrained Mapper solutions either rely on batch processing or static clustering assumptions, limiting adaptation to rapidly changing driving conditions and impeding true streaming, edge-compatible TDA.

APPROACH: We propose a streaming Mapper pipeline that maintains a dynamic cover of the data space through adaptive micro-clusters updated online with reservoir sampling to bound memory use. The algorithm incrementally builds and refines the Mapper graph by hierarchically merging clusters based on persistent homology signatures, ensuring topological features reflect temporal changes without storing full datasets. Memory usage is tightly controlled via cluster size limits and summary pruning, enabling operation within a 1GB edge memory budget under continuous sensory input.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on high-fidelity autonomous driving simulators (e.g., CARLA) generating multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, radar). Metrics include memory footprint, processing latency, and Mapper graph stability and relevance measured by correlation with driving scenario changes and downstream decision accuracy. Comparisons will be made against batch Mapper and latency-optimized but memory-unbounded Mapper baselines.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior memory-constrained Mapper implementations that either operate offline or assume static clustering, our method integrates online adaptive micro-clustering with reservoir sampling and hierarchical Mapper graph updates, enabling true streaming TDA with provable memory bounds and responsiveness to dynamic environments on edge devices.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop a streaming persistent homology framework with adaptive filtration update and parallel incremental computation to achieve ultra-low latency topological feature extraction from continuous autonomous driving video streams.

BACKGROUND: Existing persistent homology algorithms operate in batch mode or with high latency, preventing real-time topological analysis of continuous, high-frame-rate video data crucial for autonomous driving decisions. The open problem is designing a persistent homology method that incrementally updates topological summaries with minimal delay as new frames arrive, enabling timely detection of critical topological events under computational constraints.

APPROACH: We propose a streaming persistent homology algorithm that incrementally updates Vietoris–Rips filtrations using adaptive landmark selection and dynamic simplex insertion/removal, combined with parallelized reduction of boundary matrices tailored for GPU acceleration. The method incorporates a sliding time window over sensor video frames, maintaining an evolving filtration to continuously track topological features without recomputing from scratch, ensuring output latency below the frame interval.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be performed on annotated autonomous driving datasets such as KITTI and nuScenes, measuring end-to-end latency from frame acquisition to topological event output, detection accuracy of known topological changes (e.g., object boundaries, scene transitions), and robustness under varying video resolution and frame rates. Baselines include standard batch persistent homology algorithms and recent streaming topology methods without latency optimization.
